text: The video shows a normal-appearing liver and a large hiatal hernia defect, with the stomach visibly herniated into the chest cavity.
The surgeon places marking sutures at 100 centimeters and 250 centimeters from the ileocecal valve, indicating key measurement points during the procedure.
The surgeon approximates the valve located at 250 centimeters to the anterior abdominal wall, securing it in place.
The patient is positioned in reverse Trendelenburg, and the robotic surgical system is docked to begin the procedure.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to clear adhesions from the gallbladder fossa, revealing the anatomical area free of fibrous tissue.
The surgeon mobilizes the greater curvature of the stomach, starting approximately four centimeters beyond the pylorus and extending to the angle of His.
The surgeon dissects the cura of the diaphragm both anteriorly and posteriorly, fully mobilizing the defect without causing injury to the esophagus.
The surgeon positions the stomach and marks the proposed staple line with surgical ink, preparing for the next step in the procedure.
The surgeon performs a posterior dissection of the duodenum, preparing the area to accommodate a surgical staple.
The surgeon divides the duodenum using a tan, 60mm surgical stapler, visibly separating the duodenal tissue.

The surgeon places a thirty-two French bougie inside the stomach and divides the stomach near the antrum using purple forty-five millimeter staple loads without seam guards. As the stomach becomes thinner, forty-five millimeter staple loads with seam guards are used for division.
The surgeon places two plagiarized sutures of 1-0 Ticron posteriorly using a left thoracic approach, securing the tissue in the surgical field.
The surgeon approximates the anterior hiatus defect using plagiarized sutures of One-O Ticron, securing the tissue in place.
The surgeon tags the specimen with a single stitch, preparing it for extraction from the surgical field.
The surgeon removes the valve from the anterior abdominal wall at the 250-centimeter mark and approximates it to the duodenum, visually aligning the structures for connection.
The surgeon performs a running suture along the posterior aspect of the surgical site, securing the tissue layers in a continuous manner.
The surgeon creates enterotomies using hook electrocautery, followed by completion with a harmonic scalpel, visibly incising the tissue with precision.
The surgeon completes the second layer of anastomosis in a running fashion, visually securing the tissue layers together.
The surgeon completes a two-layer hand-sewn anastomosis using 3-0 absorbable V-lock sutures, without placing any muscular stitches.
The surgeon anastomoses the biliary limb to the ileum at the 100-centimeter mark using a 60-millimeter stapler, securing the connection between these structures.
The surgeon hand-sews the enterotomy using 3-0 V-lock absorbable sutures, completing the closure with precise stitching.
The surgeon closes the mesenteric defect in an extended fashion using a 3-0 V-lock non-absorbable suture, ensuring the defect is securely sealed.
The surgeon uses a tan load, sixty millimeter stapler to divide the biliary limb near the duodenal ileostomy, effectively separating it from the alimentary limb.
The surgeon oversews the stomach using absorbable V-Lock sutures in areas where reinforcement was not previously applied.
